prompt: In the most prosperous portions of the republic, it is now widely regarded as a mark of low station and degraded condition to perform any labor with one's hands. The professions of farmer, carpenter, and smith are spoken of with a condescension formerly reserved for servants. Citizens of middling means seek to distinguish themselves by avoiding all manual exertion. What effect must such a disposition have upon republican virtue and self-governance?
response:
Your observation touches upon a corruption of republican principle that I have long feared might overtake our nation, though I confess I had not anticipated it would manifest in quite this form. The tendency you describe—this disdain for honest labor and the elevation of idleness as a mark of distinction—strikes at the very foundation upon which republican government must rest.

When I consider the ancient republics, I find that their decline was invariably preceded by the growth of luxury among the citizens and a corresponding contempt for the productive arts. Rome in her glory honored the citizen-soldier who returned from victory to his plow; Rome in her decay scorned all labor save that which ministered to pleasure and ostentation. The parallel is too exact to be dismissed as mere coincidence.

The farmer who works his own soil, the artisan who shapes wood or metal by his skill, the merchant who conducts his trade with integrity—these are the men upon whom republican institutions depend. They possess what I have called the republican virtues: independence of judgment born of economic self-sufficiency, practical wisdom gained through useful labor, and that stake in the community which comes from productive contribution to its welfare. When such citizens form the majority of the electorate, they may be trusted to choose representatives who will serve the common good rather than particular interests.

But when the ideal of citizenship is divorced from productive labor—when men of middling station aspire to gentility rather than excellence in their callings—the character of the polity must necessarily change. Those who contribute nothing to the common stock of wealth and knowledge, yet who regard themselves as superior to those who do, become prey to the arts of demagogues who flatter their pretensions while serving interests hostile to republican liberty.

I observe, further, that this corruption of sentiment creates a dangerous division within the body of citizens. On one side stand those who perform the essential labors of society but find themselves despised for their useful exertions; on the other, those who have adopted the prejudices of an artificial aristocracy without possessing either its hereditary claims or its cultivated virtues. Such a division must weaken that sense of common citizenship upon which republican government depends.

The immediate practical consequence is the degradation of the electoral process itself. When honest labor is stigmatized, men of genuine merit in the productive arts will be less inclined to seek public office, leaving the field to those whose chief qualification is their success in avoiding useful employment. The representatives chosen by such a process will naturally reflect the prejudices of their constituents—preferring the appearance of gentility to the substance of public service.

Experience has shown us that republics perish not from external conquest but from internal corruption. The corruption you describe—this preference for the ornamental over the useful, for the appearance of elevation over the reality of contribution—is precisely the sort of moral decay that has preceded the fall of every free government in history. When citizens cease to honor productive labor, they have begun to dishonor the very principle that justifies their political equality.

The remedy, if one exists, must be found in education and example rather than in law. The law cannot compel men to honor what they have chosen to despise, but education might restore a proper understanding of the relationship between useful labor and civic virtue. Those who possess both wealth and wisdom must demonstrate by their conduct that true distinction lies not in the avoidance of honest work but in its skillful and conscientious performance.

I fear, however, that the tendency you describe may prove irreversible once it has taken firm hold upon the public mind. The habits of luxury and the prejudices of artificial distinction are more easily acquired than abandoned. Should this corruption of republican sentiment continue to spread, we may find that our institutions, however wisely framed, cannot preserve the liberty of a people who have forgotten the virtues upon which that liberty depends.
